trajectory.description: Starting iteration 0: Need to set up the full pipeline - model, training, prediction scripts. Using ESM2_t30_150M pretrained embeddings (frozen) + MLP regression head. Features include CLS token, global mean pool, mutation region mean pool, and one-hot encoding of mutation region (positions 561-588). This gives a strong baseline with the frozen protein language model capturing contextual information while the mutation region features provide direct mutational information important for the two_vs_many extrapolation task.

trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline val Spearman 0.8836 with frozen ESM2 features + MLP. Model overfitted (best at epoch 20). Precomputed features saved. Analysis: For two_vs_many extrapolation, need mutation-aware features (count, pairwise interactions) and better regularization. Decision: Add mutation count + per-position indicators + pairwise co-mutation indicators (407 features), use deeper MLP with batch norm, early stopping, and learning rate warmup.

trajectory.description: Observation: Evaluation failed - predict.py OOM/timeout when computing ESM2 features on full 735-AA sequences for 50K test samples. Analysis: The full-sequence ESM2 inference is too slow and memory-intensive for the eval container. Decision: Retrain using ESM2 features from mutation region subsequence only (~88 AA around positions 530-618). This makes attention O(n^2) ~70x cheaper, enabling fast inference. Combine with one-hot (560) + mutation count (1) + pairwise co-mutation (378) features.

trajectory.description: Observation: Val Spearman dropped from 0.8836 (full-seq ESM2) to 0.8806 (subseq-only ESM2). The subseq approach is necessary for fast inference but loses global context. Analysis: Adding esm2_t6_8M full-sequence features (CLS + global pool + mut region pool = 960 dims) should recover the lost global context while keeping inference fast enough for 50K test sequences. Decision: Implement dual-model feature extraction - esm2_t6_8M for full sequence + esm2_t30_150M for subsequence + one-hot + mutation features. Total features: ~3847.

trajectory.description: Observation: Evaluation failed again because predict.py runs out of time/memory during full-sequence ESM2 inference on 50K test sequences. Val Spearman was 0.8832 with dual-model approach but we can't get predictions out. Analysis: Full-sequence ESM2 inference on 735-AA sequences is too expensive for the eval container with 50K sequences. Need to avoid it entirely. Decision: Replace full-sequence features with per-position ESM2 features from the subsequence model (esm2_t30_150M). Extracting 28 position-specific representations (28*480=13,440 features) captures rich contextual information including epistatic effects, while subsequence inference remains fast (~2 min for 50K). Combine with one-hot + mutation features. Total features: 15,847.

trajectory.description: Observation: Val Spearman 0.8862 but eval keeps failing - likely OOM/timeout during esm2_t30_150M inference on 50K test sequences. Analysis: The 150M ESM model is too heavy for the eval container. Need to switch to esm2_t12_35M (35M params, 4x smaller, same 480-dim representations). Also adding PCA (512 components) to reduce per-position features from 13440 to 512 dims, cutting memory and improving generalization. Decision: Retrain with esm2_t12_35M + PCA, test predict.py locally, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Val Spearman 0.8866 vs test 0.7432 - huge extrapolation gap. Training on ≤2 mutations, testing on >2. Analysis: Model learns patterns specific to low-order mutants that don't generalize. Need to expose model to multi-mutation patterns. Decision: Add data augmentation with synthetic 3-6 mutation sequences using additive+pairwise effect estimates from training data. Also add property diff features and increase regularization.

trajectory.description: Observation: Augmentation caused catastrophic drop from 0.7432 to -0.337 test Spearman. Synthetic multi-mutation targets overestimated fitness (missing negative epistasis). Analysis: Must remove augmentation entirely. To improve extrapolation, use additive model (from single-mutant effects) as a strong prior - train on residuals so model only learns epistatic corrections. Decision: Retrain without augmentation, compute additive predictions from single-mutant effects, train model on residuals (target - additive_pred), predict as additive + model_output.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 14 completed training with additive residual approach (5 ensemble members, val Spearman 0.8642). Previous best test Spearman was 0.7432 (without additive approach). The augmentation attempt was catastrophic (-0.337). The additive residual approach should help extrapolation because the additive model from single-mutant effects generalizes naturally to multi-mutation sequences, while the neural network only learns epistatic corrections. Analysis: Must submit eval immediately to see if additive residual improves test Spearman. This is the key hypothesis - additive baseline + residual learning should extrapolate much better to >2 mutation sequences. Decision: Submit eval now, then analyze results to determine next steps.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best test Spearman is 0.7432 from basic MLP. Additive residual approach failed because NN corrections didn't generalize to >2 mutations. Current model (iter 17) hasn't been tested yet. Analysis: For extrapolation from ≤2 to >2 mutations, we should trust the additive model more for higher mutation counts since it naturally extends. The MLP likely degrades for out-of-distribution sequences. Decision: (1) Submit current model eval to get baseline, (2) Modify predict.py to blend MLP + additive predictions with mutation-count-dependent alpha, (3) Also add ridge regression as an additional model for better extrapolation. Start with the blending approach since it doesn't require retraining.

Decision: Fix predict.py to convert float32 to float, then resubmit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best test Spearman is 0.7432 (iter 9, basic MLP without additive features/sample weights). Current checkpoint trained with additive features + sample weights gives 0.5538. The additive features and sample weights are hurting extrapolation. Analysis: The additive feature adds information that helps val but hurts test because it reinforces training distribution patterns. Sample weights emphasizing higher mutation counts cause the model to overfit to the few high-mutation training examples. Decision: Retrain with clean approach - no additive features, no sample weights, same as iter 9. Also try slightly more regularization (higher dropout) to improve extrapolation.

Analysis: Ridge regression (linear model) should extrapolate better because it can't learn complex feature interactions that are specific to ≤2 mutations. Also trying Ridge on one-hot only (pure additive model) which should generalize best to >2 mutations. Decision: Train both Ridge models, save both in checkpoint, submit eval for the full-feature Ridge first.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best test Spearman is 0.7432 (iter 9, esm2_t12_35M MLP). The 150M model got only 0.5282 on test. Various extrapolation improvements (augmentation, additive residual, Ridge, blending) have all failed. The 35M model clearly generalizes better than 150M for this extrapolation task. Analysis: The iter 9 model used PCA dimensionality reduction which was removed in later iterations. PCA reduces overfitting by projecting high-dimensional features to a lower-dimensional space, which should help extrapolation. Also, the tabular features (one-hot, properties) likely encourage memorization of training patterns. Decision: Retrain with esm2_t12_35M, ESM-only features (cls+mean+max), PCA to 256 dims, simpler MLP [512,256], and stronger regularization (weight_decay=1e-3). This combines the best-performing model (35M) with PCA regularization and removes potentially harmful tabular features.
